coredata.dc:title: pH-induced highly sensitive fluorescence detection of urea and urease based on carbon dots-based nanohybrids
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: An, Jia
Hu, Yongqin
Yang, Da
Han, Yaqin
Zhang, Jiajing
Liu, Yufei
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CDs) have become one of the most popular fluorescent materials due to their intriguing performance, which are favored by many fields. However, it is difficult to synthesize CDs with high quantum yield by the simple synthesis methods. In this paper, we fabricated CDs- silicon (SiO2) spheres composites via a versatile hydrothermal route. The prepared BCD-SiO2 composites exhibited an approximately 10-fold increase in the fluorescence intensity over that of BCDs. At the same time, the purification path was simplified by the facile separation of SiO2 spheres. The prepared BCD-SiO2 composites were used to fabricate a special sensing platform for the ultrasensitive detection of urea and urease, with detection limits of 1.67 μM and 0.002 mg/mL, respectively. Furthermore, this strategy was successfully applied to the detection of real samples. This result shows that as-prepared BCDs-SiO2 composites are promising for broad application to biological analysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Silicon spheres
Fluorescence enhancement
Urea and urease

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Thanks for the test platform provided by the Analytical and Testing Center of Chongqing University and Beijing Zhongkebaice Technology Service Co. Ltd.

This work is supported by Chongqing Entrepreneurship and Innovation Support Program (CX201803), China Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (Grant No. 2019CDQYGD020, 2019CDCGGD304 and 2020CDJGFCG006), the National Key Research and Development Program of China (Grant No. 2016YFC0101100 and 2016YFE0125200).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) SEM image of BCDs-SiO2 composites; b) TEM image of BCDs-SiO2 composites (the inset graph is corresponding size distribution plot); c) XRD patterns of BCDs-SiO2 composites; d) XPS patterns of BCDs-SiO2 composites.

a) Fluorescence spectrum (blue line) and absorbance spectra (black line) of BCDs and BCDs-SiO2 composites (the inset graph is BCDs and BCDs-SiO2 composites solution under UV-light); b) FTIR spectrum of BCDs and BCDs-SiO2 composites; c) fluorescence spectrum of composite solutions after storage; d) fluorescence spectrum for composite solutions in environments with different pH values.
Normalized fluorescence intensity of BCDs-SiO2 composites towards different a) reaction times; b) DA concentrations; c) urease concentrations; d) reaction temperature.

a) Fluorescence spectrum of BCDs-SiO2 composites towards different concentration of urea; b) The linear relationship between I / I0 and the different concentrations of urea; c) The interference studies of the BCDs-SiO2 composites toward urea; d) The photo of interference studies samples under UV-light.
a) Fluorescence spectrum of BCDs-SiO2 composites towards different concentration of urease; b) The linear relationship between I / I0 and the different concentrations of urease.
Processing diagram for the synthesis of BCDs-SiO2 composites and urea/urease detection principle.
Performance comparison of different urea sensors.
Detection of urea in real samples.

head.ce:title: pH-induced highly sensitive fluorescence detection of urea and urease based on carbon dots-based nanohybrids

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Optoelectronic Technology & Systems (Chongqing University), Ministry of Education, Chongqing 400044, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Optoelectronic Technology & Systems

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Optoelectronic Technology & Systems (Chongqing University), Ministry of Education, Chongqing 400044, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Centre for NanoHealth, College of Engineering, Swansea University, Singleton Park, Swansea SA2 8PP, UK
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Centre for NanoHealth, College of Engineering, Swansea University, Singleton Park, Swansea SA2 8PP, UK

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Key Laboratory of Optoelectronic Technology & Systems (Chongqing University), Ministry of Education, Chongqing 400044, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Optoelectronic Technology & Systems

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CDs) have become one of the most popular fluorescent materials due to their intriguing performance, which are favored by many fields. However, it is difficult to synthesize CDs with high quantum yield by the simple synthesis methods. In this paper, we fabricated CDs- silicon (SiO2) spheres composites via a versatile hydrothermal route. The prepared BCD-SiO2 composites exhibited an approximately 10-fold increase in the fluorescence intensity over that of BCDs. At the same time, the purification path was simplified by the facile separation of SiO2 spheres. The prepared BCD-SiO2 composites were used to fabricate a special sensing platform for the ultrasensitive detection of urea and urease, with detection limits of 1.67 μM and 0.002 mg/mL, respectively. Furthermore, this strategy was successfully applied to the detection of real samples. This result shows that as-prepared BCDs-SiO2 composites are promising for broad application to biological analysis.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CDs), as a fantastic material have drawn considerable attention due to their superior properties, such as tunable photoluminescence (PL), high biocompatibility, hyper-stability, low cost and so on [1–5]. Generally, the preparation method of CDs involved laser ablation, hydrothermal synthesis, wet oxidation, and chemical oxidation method. The advantages of CDs have been exploited in application fields ranging from bioimaging, analytical determination, biomedical and therapeutic analyses, and photocatalysis to photoelectric devices [6–11]. After a long period of development, synthesis methods of CDs tend to be simple and efficient. Unquestionable, hydrothermal synthesis method has become a favored method in recent years [12–17]. However, a majority of methods used for synthesizing CDs result in a low fluorescence quantum yield (QY), warranting subsequent purification that is time-consuming and expensive. Thus, many researchers endeavor to improve the efficiency of synthesis and purification. Song et al. [18] synthesized CQDs@SiO2 by a reversed-phase microemulsion method, which not only keeps the characteristics of CQDs unchanged, but also prevents the aggregation-induced fluorescence quenching of CQDs. Tan et al. [19] fabricated composites by encapsulating CDs and upconversion nanoparticles in silicon (SiO2) spheres for application to ink-jet printing barcodes. The synthesized composites promote photobleaching resistance and fluorescence under acidic conditions. Xu et al. [20] successfully prepared CDs-SiO2 spheres by a co-hydrolysis method and used the as-synthesized composites to detect the SFTSV virus. These synthesis methods are simple and easy to perform, and the excellent fluorescence performance and stability of the resulting CD-SiO2 composites considerably improve detection sensitivity.

Urea, a crucial nitrogenous compound, not only as a high concentration of nitrogen fertilizer generally used in agricultural production, but also as one of the primary metabolites in living organisms [21,22]. However, abnormal urea levels will cause soil acidification and serious diseases, such as kidney failure [23,24]. Therefore, real-time monitoring of the urea content in human blood and urine is essential for the early diagnosis of kidney diseases. Urease is an enzyme that plays a critical role in urea transformation. The enzymatic reaction product is a nitrogen source that is available to plants, and urease activity can be used as an indicator of the nitrogen supply capacity of soil [25,26]. Thus, it is important to fabricate a fast and sensitive urease detection method.

In this work, we synthesized BCDs-SiO2 composites by a one-pot hydrothermal method, in which SiO2 spheres were used as the carrier on which BCDs were grown. The prepared BCDs-SiO2 composites have super bright blue fluorescence, good water solubility, and light stability. The SiO2 spheres enable the composites to be easily functionalized, facilitating a variety of applications. This BCD-SiO2 composite material and dopamine (DA) were used to develop a smart strategy to simultaneously detect urea and urease (See Scheme 1 ). More importantly, this method has significant potential for the detection of urea in real samples.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was investigated using a Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN transmission electron microscope (FEI, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was made on an ESCALAB250Xi spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific). UV–vis spectra were recorded with a BioTek microplate reader (ELx800, USA). FL measurements were obtained by using a Hitachi F-4700 fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi. Ltd., Japan). XRD patterns were recorded on a Bruck D8 ADVANCE. FTIR spectra were collected using a Thermo Scientific FT-IR spectrophotometer (Nicolet 380 FT-IR). The fluorescence lifetime and fluorescence quantum yield were obtained by using Edinburgh Instruments (FLS1000).

Tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS), dopamine hydrochloride (DA), glycine (Gly), alanine (Ala), phenylalanine (Phe), histidine (His), glucose (Glu), threonine (Thr), and urea were purchased from Aldrich Chemical. Urease was purchased from Shanghai Yuanye Bio-Technology Co., Ltd. NaCl, KCl, MgCl2, ethanol, ammonia solution, citric acid (CA), ethylenediamine (EDA) and all other reagents were analytical grade. Nanopure water (18.2 MΩ; Millpore Co., USA) was used throughout the experiment. The serum samples were obtained from a healthy female volunteer.

The SiO2 spheres were prepared using the improved Stöber method. Liquid A was composed of 20 mL of ethanol, 0.3 mL of ammonia, and 3 mL of ultrapure water. Liquid B consisted of 5 mL of ethanol and 1 mL of TEOS. Liquid A and liquid B were quickly mixed and then stirred at room temperature for 4 h at a speed of 400 rpm. The samples were collected, centrifuged several times, and resuspended in ultrapure water for later use.

The BCDs-SiO2 composites were prepared by a one-pot method. Ten milliliters of a 1 mM CA solution, 10 mL of the SiO2 solution, and 670 μL of an EDA solution were mixed evenly and then ultrasonicated for 5 min. Transfer the mixture to a Tefl-on-lined autoclave chamber and reacted at 180 °C for 5 h. After the reaction was completed, the BCDs adhering to the SiO2 surface were washed away by centrifugation, and the sample was resuspended in ultrapure water for later use.

All experiments were carried out in a phosphate buffer (PB) solution (pH 7.2, 0.01 M). Typically, 10 μL of the BCDs-SiO2 composites, 50 μL of DA (0.05 M), and 5 μL urease (75 mg/mL) were dispersed in 190 μL of PB 7.2, to which 50 μL of solutions of different urea concentrations were added. The samples were incubated at 37 °C for 40 min, then collected the fluorescence spectrum with excitation wavelength of 380 nm.

Experiments were carried out to determine the urea selectivity under the same conditions as those used for urea detection. Interference experiments were performed using 0.054 M Na+, K+, and Mg 2+ and 0.021 M Ala, Phe, His, Gly, Thr, and Leu. The concentration of added urea in the control experiment was 0.002 M.

All urease detection experiments were performed in a PB 7.2 solution. Typically, 10 μL of the BCD-SiO2 composites, 50 μL of DA (0.05 M), and 50 μL of urea (0.5 M) were dispersed into 190 μL of PB 7.2, to which 5 μL of solutions with different urease concentrations were added. The samples were incubated at 37 °C for 40 min, and the fluorescence spectrum was then collected at an excitation wavelength of 380 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of urea in the real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The serum samples were first diluted 50-fold with the PB 7.2 solution, and urea at different concentrations was then added to configure standard samples. The solutions were then analyzed by the prepared BCDs-SiO2 composites. All the experimental conditions were the same as previously mentioned.

The BCD-SiO2 composites can be synthesized by a universal method that only involves a hydrothermal reaction. The resultant sample can be purified by simple centrifugation. Demonstrated the modified method used to prepare BCDs-SiO2 composites has unique advantages, such as being simple and easy to perform, being suitable for mass production, which can obtain a large yield and the subsequent purification process is uncomplicated. The morphology and structure of BCDs-SiO2 composites were characterized by SEM and TEM. As shown in Fig. 1 a, SEM image indicates BCDs-SiO2 composites have a uniform morphology and particle size. Closer inspection shows that the surface of the SiO2 spheres os not smooth, some spikes were covering the surface of the SiO2 spheres, giving the whole SiO2 spheres a rough surface structure. Meanwhile, the SEM image (Fig. S1) of SiO2 spheres shows a smooth surface. The implication is that BCDs is growing on the surface of the silicon sphere. Similarly, TEM characterization can also prove this result. Fig. 1b also shows that, unlike the surface of the SiO2 spheres (Fig. S2), the surface of the BCDs-SiO2 composites is densely covered with bright white dots, implying that the BCDs are attached to the surface of the SiO2 spheres. Meanwhile, TEM shows that the as-prepared BCDs-SiO2 are monodispersed with an average particle size of approximately 59.5 nm. Furthermore, the BCDs that were not loaded on the SiO2 spheres were also characterized by TEM (Fig. S3), it can be seen that they have good dispersion, uniform morphology and an average particle size of 1.88 nm.

In order to further prove the successful preparation of the BCDs-SiO2 composites, XRD and XPS characterization were investigated. The XRD pattern is depicted in Fig. 1c. It reveals that the diffraction peak of BCDs-SiO2 composites corresponds to the diffraction peak of BCDs, which proves that BCDs were successfully loaded on the SiO2 spheres. The full spectrum of XPS (Fig. 1d) shows the presence of Si, C, N, and O in BCDs-SiO2 composites. In addition, C1s XPS spectra of BCDs and BCDs-SiO2 composites are displayed in Fig. S2. The CS composites shows typical peaks at 284.5, 285.8 and 287.5 eV are assigned to CC/CC, CN and COH [17,27], respectively, confirming presence BCDs in CS composites. Additionally, The N1s, O1s and Si 2p XPS (Fig. S4) of BCDs-SiO2 composites indicated the presence of CNC, OH, CO, SiOC, SiOx bonds on the BCDs-SiO2 composites.

Moreover, the optical properties of the resultant BCDs-SiO2 composites were determined from photoluminescence (PL) spectra, UV–vis spectra and the photoluminescence quantum yields (QYs). The PL spectra (Fig. 2 a) indicates the emission peak of BCDs-SiO2 composites (445 nm) compared with free BCDs (444 nm) has an insignificant redshift and the Full Wave at Half Maximum (FWHM) narrows slightly. This result suggests that the SiO2 spheres matrix does not affect the fluorescence emission of the BCDs. On the other hand, Fig. 2a shows BCDs absorption peak at 344 nm, whereas the BCDs-SiO2 composites exhibit broad UV–vis absorption. This broad absorption may be due to the strong scattering of the SiO2 spheres [28]. The inset photo shows that BCDs and BCDs-SiO2 composites had the same bright blue fluorescence. Surprisingly, the fluorescence intensity of the BCDs-SiO2 composites was significantly higher than that of free BCDs. Owing to the strong scattering of SiO2 spheres, the relative quantum yield calculation method cannot be used to measure QYs. The absolute quantum yields of the BCDs and BCDs-SiO2 composites were determined to be 5% and 40%, respectively, and the fluorescence intensity of the composites was dramatically enhanced over that of the BCDs by approximately 10 times. The result is speculating like that BCDs-SiO2 composites gathered many BCDs onto the surface of SiO2 spheres, which not only enhances the fluorescence intensity of a single particle, but also inhibits the self-quenching of the BCDs [29]. Furthermore, the surface functional groups of BCDs-SiO2 composites were investigated by FTIR (Fig. 2b). The results reveal the existence of many hydrophilic functional groups on the surface of BCDs-SiO2 composites.

Miraculously, it was found that the aqueous solution of BCDs-SiO2 composites still retained strong fluorescence after 12 months of storage. Thus, to verify the PL stability of the BCDs-SiO2 composite, it was stored for three months and occasionally sampled to collect fluorescence data. The results (Fig. 2c) prove BCDs-SiO2 composites have remarkable PL stability. Furthermore, the pH stability and of BCDs-SiO2 composites was also studied (Fig. 2d), and it was found that the fluorescence intensity remained stable in environments with different pH values. Similarly, the effects of ionic strength on the fluorescence (Fig. S5) were also negligible. Therefore, all the aforementioned improvements in the performance of BCDs-SiO2 composites are beneficial for future applications.

As well-known, fluorescence materials could be quenched based on redox-active of (DA). In order to verify the feasibility of the developed detection strategy, the effects of DA on the BCDs-SiO2 composites were analyzed under different pH conditions. Configured a series of Britton-Robinson (BR) buffers with different pH values. Figure S6 illustrates the fluorescence intensity changes of the BCDs-SiO2/DA system at different pH values. It found that with pH in the range of 8–12, DA gradually quenched the fluorescence intensity of the BCDs-SiO2 composites. The quenching mechanism of DA on fluorescence was that, when the pH value from 8 to 12, DA will be oxidized to its quinone structure, and the quinone structure as an electron acceptor that then further quenched the fluorescence of the BCDs-SiO2 composites [30,31].
(1)
Urea +
H 2
O
→ Urease
H C
O
3
-
+ O
H
-
+ 2 N
H
4
+

Furthermore, as equation (1) [32] shown, urea hydrolysis produces OH–, which increases the pH of the surrounding environment. Therefore, the BCDs-SiO2/DA system can be utilized to detect urea and urease. Taking into account that urea hydrolysis yields a series of products produced, we systematically analyzed the effects of potential distractors (urea, urease, NH4 +, and HCO3 –) on the fluorescence of BCDs-SiO2 composites. As shown in Fig. S7, the presence of these interferers alone does not significantly influence the fluorescence of the BCDs-SiO2 composites. Therefore, it further demostrates the feasibility of the detection system.

In order to improve the urea detection sensitivity, it is necessary to optimize the detection conditions. The Fig. 3 a shows the trend in the reaction time for the BCDs-SiO2/DA system to detect urea, it can be found that the fluorescence intensity decreases sharply at the beginning of the reaction, but changes little beyond a reaction time of 35 min. Therefore, 35 min was determined to be the incubation time for urea detection. Meanwhile, the DA concentration in the system was analyzed (Fig. 3b), and it was found that when the DA concentration was 8.3 mM, it was most beneficial for detection, and that an excessively high DA concentration resulted in missing detection data. It can be seen from Fig. 3c when the urease concentration was 1.25 mg/mL, fluorescence intensity was reduced by 80%, indicating that this urease concentration was sufficient for urea hydrolysis. Furthermore, we studied the effect of temperature on the detection system and found that the fluorescence intensity gradually decreased as the temperature increased from 20 to 45° C (Fig. 3d) because increasing the temperature helped accelerate the hydrolysis reaction. Considering the urease activity, 37° C was determined as the detection temperature.

Similarly, fixed the urea concentration of 83.3 mM, utilized the BCDs-SiO2 composites to detect different concentrations of urease (0, 0.002, 0.021, 0.042, 0.17, 0.33, 0.5, 0.67, 0.83, 1, 1.25 mg/mL). The results showed that the fluorescence intensity decreased with the increase of urease concentration (Fig. 5 a). When the urease concentration in the range of 0–0.17 mg/ mL, it shows a good relationship between fluorescence intensity ratio I/I0 and urease concentration (Fig. 5b). The regression equation was found to be Y = -0.74 [C urease] + 0.55 (R2 = 0.999). Therefore, the detection limit for urease was 0.002 mg/mL.

Certainly, in order to verify the feasibility of the constructed BCDs-SiO2 composites sensor platform for detection in real samples, human serum samples were collected and used to study the quantitative detection of urea. A certain amount of urea was added to prepare the standard sample. The results are shown in Table. 2 . the coverage was 99–101%, RSD ≤ 3.6. These experimental results reveal that the as-prepared BCDs-SiO2 composites has the potential to be applied to the detection of urea in real samples.

In conclusion, a simple strategy was proposed to synthesize BCDs-SiO2 composites via an in situ hydrothermal method. The characterization results indicate that the synthesized BCDs-SiO2 composites exhibit excellent optical stability and could realize 10-fold enhancement of the fluorescence of BCDs. Furthermore, the preparation method also simplifies the purification process of traditional CDs, resulting in the advantages of large scale generation and low cost. Moreover, we fabricated a sensing platform for the detection of urea and urease with above BCDs-SiO2 composites. The sensor shows high sensitivity and specificity for the detection of urea and urease, with the detection limits of 1.67 μM and 0.002 mg/mL, respectively. The results demonstrate that the proposed strategy is beneficial for future biological applications.

This work is supported by Chongqing Entrepreneurship and Innovation Support Program (CX201803), China Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (Grant No. 2019CDQYGD020, 2019CDCGGD304 and 2020CDJGFCG006), the National Key Research and Development Program of China (Grant No. 2016YFC0101100 and 2016YFE0125200).

Jia An: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Writing – original draft. Yongqin Hu: Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Da Yang: Investigation, Resources. Yaqin Han: Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Jiajing Zhang: Investigation, Resources. Yufei Liu: Conceptualization, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Thanks for the test platform provided by the Analytical and Testing Center of Chongqing University and Beijing Zhongkebaice Technology Service Co., Ltd.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2021.120705.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
